codeSnapshot.label: data_type_casting.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv('transactions.csv')

# ── 1. Audit dtypes ──
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.memory_usage(deep=True).sum() / 1e6, 'MB')

# ── 2. Common type coercions ──
# String → numeric (coerce turns unparseable values to NaN)
df['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(df['amount'], errors='coerce')

# String → datetime
df['created_at'] = pd.to_datetime(df['created_at'], format='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S', utc=True)

# String → categorical (reduces memory for low-cardinality columns)
df['country'] = df['country'].astype('category')   # e.g., 200 unique values
df['status'] = pd.Categorical(df['status'],
    categories=['pending', 'active', 'churned'], ordered=True)

# ── 3. Downcast numeric types to save memory ──
# int64 (8 bytes) → int16 (2 bytes) if values fit in [-32768, 32767]
df['age'] = pd.to_numeric(df['age'], downcast='integer')   # auto-downcast
df['score'] = df['score'].astype(np.float32)  # float64 → float32 (4 bytes)

# Convenience: downcast all numeric columns
for col in df.select_dtypes(include='integer'):
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], downcast='integer')
for col in df.select_dtypes(include='float'):
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], downcast='float')

print('After optimization:', df.memory_usage(deep=True).sum() / 1e6, 'MB')

# ── 4. Boolean columns ──
df['is_fraud'] = df['is_fraud'].astype(bool)  # 1/0 → True/False

# ── 5. Checking for silent type errors ──
# pd.read_csv infers types — '3.0' may be read as float when int is correct
# '2024-01-01' stays as object unless you specify parse_dates
df2 = pd.read_csv('data.csv',
    dtype={'user_id': str, 'amount': float},
    parse_dates=['created_at'],
    date_format='%Y-%m-%d')

eli3: Every column in a dataset has a type: a number, a category, a date, or free text. Data type casting means converting a column from the type it was read in as to the type it actually should be. The most common problem is that CSV files have no type system — everything arrives as text — so pandas has to guess, and guesses can be wrong.

A phone number read as an integer is a bug: leading zeros vanish, arithmetic makes no sense. A column of 'yes'/'no' stored as a string wastes 5× more memory than it needs to and prevents logical operations. A date stored as a string can't be sorted correctly or have time-delta computations applied to it.

Beyond correctness, type casting dramatically reduces memory usage. Storing age (values 0–120) as int64 (8 bytes) instead of int8 (1 byte) wastes 8× memory. On a 100-million-row dataset this matters a lot: it can mean the difference between fitting the data in RAM and having to use chunked processing.

whenWhy.title: Post-ingestion data validation
whenWhy.body: Raw CSV/JSON data lands in pandas with inferred types, often incorrectly. Standard first step after reading: audit dtypes with df.dtypes, check for object columns that should be numeric or datetime, and apply explicit casts. Always cast at ingestion time, not downstream — silent type mismatches (a string 'N/A' in a numeric column read as NaN only after coerce) propagate as data quality bugs.

whenWhy.title: Memory optimization for large datasets
whenWhy.body: Pandas defaults to int64 and float64 for all numeric columns. Downcasting to the smallest type that can hold the data range can reduce DataFrame memory 2–4×. For a 10-column, 50M-row DataFrame with all int64 columns: 50M × 10 × 8 bytes = 4GB. Downcasting to int16 where possible: ~1GB. This often makes the difference between fitting in RAM and hitting a memory limit in a notebook or pipeline.

whenWhy.title: Enabling type-specific operations
whenWhy.body: Pandas datetime64[ns] columns enable vectorized time operations: df['created_at'].dt.year, df.resample('M'), pd.Grouper(freq='W'). Categorical dtype enables ordered comparison (df[df['status'] > 'pending']), memory efficiency (stores integer codes internally), and fast groupby. Neither is available if the column is stored as object (string). Correct typing unlocks the full pandas API.

deepDiveIntro: Pandas stores data as numpy arrays under the hood. The object dtype — the fallback type for columns pandas cannot categorise — stores Python object pointers rather than raw values, so even a column of short strings consumes about 50 bytes per element. Converting a low-cardinality string column to categorical dtype stores the unique values once and uses 1–2 bytes per row for an integer code, giving 10–100x memory savings. [1]

Type inference when reading a CSV is heuristic: pandas samples up to 100 rows, guesses the type, and applies it to all rows. A single non-numeric value like 'N/A' in an otherwise numeric column forces the entire column to object; an integer column with any decimal values becomes float64. The fix is simple: always specify dtype and parse_dates explicitly in pd.read_csv() for production pipelines rather than relying on inference. [1]

deepDive.heading: Numeric Type Hierarchy and Downcasting
deepDive.body: Pandas defaults to 8-byte integers and 8-byte floats for all numeric columns. Downcasting to the smallest type that fits the data range can halve or quarter memory usage with no loss of information. Calling pd.to_numeric with downcast='integer' automatically selects the smallest safe integer type. Float16 has only about 3 significant digits of precision — avoid it for financial amounts or ML gradient computations; float32 is generally fine for model features and cuts memory in half compared to float64. [1]

deepDive.heading: Categorical Dtype
deepDive.body: Categorical dtype stores a column as a lookup table of unique values plus a compact array of integer codes, one per row. For a column with a million rows and 200 unique country names, object dtype uses around 50 MB while categorical uses around 1 MB — a 50x saving. The savings compound when you have many such columns. Ordered categoricals additionally support comparison operators, so you can ask 'which rows have status greater than pending' directly without manual mapping. Groupby and sort operations are also faster on categoricals than on object columns. [1]

deepDive.heading: DateTime Handling
deepDive.body: Pandas represents timestamps internally as 64-bit integers counting nanoseconds since 1970, enabling vectorised date arithmetic and resampling. Providing an explicit format string to pd.to_datetime is 10–20x faster than letting pandas guess the format. Always store timestamps in UTC and convert to local time only for display — mixing timezone-aware and timezone-naive columns causes errors. Dates stored as strings sort lexicographically, which is only correct for ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD); any other string format will sort incorrectly without conversion. [1]

youtubeLabel: Pandas Data Types & Memory Optimization — Keith Galli
takeaways: pandas defaults to object (string), int64, float64 — always audit dtypes after read and apply explicit casts. [1]
Categorical dtype: 10–100× memory savings for low-cardinality string columns; enables fast groupby and ordered comparisons. [1]
pd.to_numeric(series, downcast='integer'/'float'): auto-selects smallest safe type. float32 is generally sufficient for ML features. [1]
Specify dtype dict and parse_dates in pd.read_csv() for production pipelines — never rely on type inference. [1]
datetime64[ns]: store in UTC, parse with explicit format string for 10–20× faster parsing vs auto-inference. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle mixed-type columns (e.g., a column with both strings and numbers)?
edgeCases.a: Mixed-type columns (object dtype) arise when a numeric column contains sentinel strings like 'N/A', '-', or 'unknown'. pd.to_numeric(col, errors='coerce') converts parseable values to numeric and turns everything else into NaN. After coercion, audit the resulting NaN positions: if they correlate with a specific value pattern, you've discovered a non-standard missing value encoding that needs cleaning. pd.read_csv(na_values=['-', 'N/A', 'unknown', '?']) handles this at read time. For multiple sentinel values, pass na_values per column using a dict.

edgeCases.q: When should you use nullable integer types (pd.Int64Dtype()) vs numpy int64?
edgeCases.a: Standard numpy int64 cannot represent NaN — it's a floating-point concept. If a column has missing integers, pandas converts the entire column to float64 (to accommodate NaN), losing int semantics. pandas nullable integer types (Int8, Int16, Int32, Int64 — capital I) use a separate boolean mask for missingness, allowing integer arrays with NaN values. Use nullable integers when: (1) You need to preserve integer dtype through missing value operations, (2) You use pandas' groupby/merge and need correct integer joins without float precision issues. Downside: nullable integers are slower than numpy int64 for arithmetic (~2× slower in benchmarks), so prefer numpy types when there are no missing values.

sources.label: McKinney, W. — Python for Data Analysis, 3rd Ed., Ch. 7 & Appendix A (O'Reilly, 2022)
